methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $1.0B - $23.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 25.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Cerebras
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $23B

private.name: Ouster
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1.5B

private.name: Aeva Technologies
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Public), post-money $1B+

data.narrative:
Credo Semiconductor is a publicly listed semiconductor company that completed its IPO in 2022 and currently trades at a valuation exceeding $5B. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable universe of 3 public-stage peers in the Chips & Semiconductors sector, supplemented by a broader reference set of 8 private competitors including Cerebras, Ouster, and Aeva Technologies.

The peer-median method is the primary applicable triangulation approach. The median valuation across 3 same-sector, same-stage comparables—Cerebras ($23.0B), Ouster ($1.5B), and Aeva Technologies ($1.0B)—yields $1.5B. This cohort exhibits substantial dispersion, with a range spanning $1.0B to $23.0B, reflecting high volatility in public semiconductor equity valuations. The public-comp anchoring method using sector price-to-sales multiples (median 25.3x across ALAB, NVDA, and AMD) cannot be applied, as the target company's revenue is not on file.

The triangulated valuation range supported by available data is $1.3B to $1.7B, with a median point of $1.5B. The substantial gap between this derived range and Credo's stated $5B+ valuation warrants scrutiny; key uncertainties include whether undisclosed revenue or proprietary metrics justify the premium, and whether public market repricing has occurred since IPO. The single-method foundation and wide dispersion among peers introduce material estimation risk.
